Hi, I was out sick from my classes on Friday, so I'm trying to figure out what I need to study for my Calculus mid-term next week. My friend from class sent me an audio recording of Professor Willowbrook giving out the recommended reading for the test, but my headphones are broken :(

¬(A ∧ B) ↔ (¬A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A ∨ B) ↔ (¬A ∧ ¬B)
(A → B) ↔ (¬B → ¬A)
(A → B) ↔ (¬A ∨ B)
(¬A → B) ↔ (A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A → B) ↔ (A ∧ ¬B)

Which of the above is not logically equivalent to the rest? Provide the full statement that doesn't fit.

Consider the following six logical statements:
¬(A ∧ B) ↔ (¬A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A ∨ B) ↔ (¬A ∧ ¬B)
(A → B) ↔ (¬B → ¬A)
(A → B) ↔ (¬A ∨ B)
(¬A → B) ↔ (A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A → B) ↔ (A ∧ ¬B)

One of these is not logically equivalent to the others. Which one? Write out the full statement that does not belong.

Below are six propositional logic biconditionals:
¬(A ∧ B) ↔ (¬A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A ∨ B) ↔ (¬A ∧ ¬B)
(A → B) ↔ (¬B → ¬A)
(A → B) ↔ (¬A ∨ B)
(¬A → B) ↔ (A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A → B) ↔ (A ∧ ¬B)

Five of these are logically equivalent to one another, but one is not. Identify and write the full statement that is the odd one out.

Examine the six logical equivalences listed below:
¬(A ∧ B) ↔ (¬A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A ∨ B) ↔ (¬A ∧ ¬B)
(A → B) ↔ (¬B → ¬A)
(A → B) ↔ (¬A ∨ B)
(¬A → B) ↔ (A ∨ ¬B)
¬(A → B) ↔ (A ∧ ¬B)

Which single statement among them is not logically equivalent to the remaining five? Provide that complete statement.

You are a telecommunications engineer who wants to build cell phone towers on a stretch of road. In the reference file is a layout of the road and nearby houses. Each dash, "-", is a marker indicating a mile. Each capital H indicates a house located next to a mile marker, appearing above or below the stretch of road. Each cell phone tower can cover houses located next to the road within a 4-mile radius. Find the minimum number of cell phone towers needed to cover all houses next to the road. Your answer should be a positive numerical integer value.

As a telecom engineer, you need to place cell towers along a road segment. The attached file contains a diagram of the road and surrounding houses. Each "-" character marks one mile along the road, and each "H" (uppercase) denotes a house positioned beside that mile marker, either above or below the road. A single cell tower can serve all houses within 4 miles along the road. What is the fewest number of towers required to provide coverage to every house shown in the reference file? Provide your answer as a positive integer.

You are planning cell tower placement for a stretch of road. Refer to the provided file for the road layout: each dash ("-") represents a one-mile marker, and each capital letter H marks a house adjacent to the road at that mile marker (positioned above or below the road line). Every tower you place covers all houses within a 4-mile radius along the road. Determine the minimum number of cell phone towers needed so that no house is left uncovered. Your answer must be a positive whole number.

A telecommunications engineer must deploy the minimum number of cell towers to cover all houses along a road. The reference file shows the road map: dashes ("-") denote mile markers, and capital H's represent houses sitting next to the road at those markers, either above or below it. Each tower's coverage extends 4 miles in either direction along the road. Using the layout in the provided file, what is the smallest integer number of towers required to cover every house?

I'm making a grocery list for my mom, but she's a professor of botany and she's a real stickler when it comes to categorizing things. I need to add different foods to different categories on the grocery list, but if I make a mistake, she won't buy anything inserted in the wrong category. Here's the list I have so far:

milk, eggs, flour, whole bean coffee, Oreos, sweet potatoes, fresh basil, plums, green beans, rice, corn, bell pepper, whole allspice, acorns, broccoli, celery, zucchini, lettuce, peanuts

I need to make headings for the fruits and vegetables. Could you please create a list of just the vegetables from my list? If you could do that, then I can figure out how to categorize the rest of the list into the appropriate categories. But remember that my mom is a real stickler, so make sure that no botanical fruits end up on the vegetable list, or she won't get them when she's at the store. Please alphabetize the list of vegetables, and place each item in a comma separated list.
final_answer: broccoli, celery, fresh basil, lettuce, sweet potatoes

question:
I'm putting together a grocery list for my mom, who is a botany professor and extremely particular about how foods are categorized. She won't purchase anything placed in the wrong category. Here is my current list:

milk, eggs, flour, whole bean coffee, Oreos, sweet potatoes, fresh basil, plums, green beans, rice, corn, bell pepper, whole allspice, acorns, broccoli, celery, zucchini, lettuce, peanuts

I need to separate these into fruits and vegetables. Can you pull out only the vegetables from this list? Keep in mind that my mom is a strict botanist, so nothing that is botanically a fruit should appear on the vegetable list, or she won't buy it. Please present the vegetables in alphabetical order as a comma-separated list.
final_answer: broccoli, celery, fresh basil, lettuce, sweet potatoes

question:
My mom is a botany professor who is very strict about correct food categorization — if anything is in the wrong category, she'll refuse to buy it. I'm organizing a grocery list and need to identify the vegetables from the following items:

milk, eggs, flour, whole bean coffee, Oreos, sweet potatoes, fresh basil, plums, green beans, rice, corn, bell pepper, whole allspice, acorns, broccoli, celery, zucchini, lettuce, peanuts

Please list only the true vegetables (excluding any items that are botanical fruits) from the list above, sorted alphabetically and formatted as a comma-separated list.
final_answer: broccoli, celery, fresh basil, lettuce, sweet potatoes

question:
I'm helping my mom — a botany professor who insists on scientifically accurate food categorization — build a grocery list. She won't buy any item placed under the wrong heading. Here are the foods I have:

milk, eggs, flour, whole bean coffee, Oreos, sweet potatoes, fresh basil, plums, green beans, rice, corn, bell pepper, whole allspice, acorns, broccoli, celery, zucchini, lettuce, peanuts

For the vegetables section, could you identify which of these items are true vegetables, making sure to exclude anything that is botanically classified as a fruit? Return the vegetables in alphabetical order as a comma-separated list.
final_answer: broccoli, celery, fresh basil, lettuce, sweet potatoes

In the fictional language of Tizin, basic sentences are arranged with the Verb first, followed by the direct object, followed by the subject of the sentence. I want to express my love for apples to my Tizin friend. 

The word that indicates oneself is "Pa" is the nominative form, "Mato" is the accusative form, and "Sing" is the genitive form. 

The root verb that indicates an intense like for something is "Maktay". When it is used in the present, it is used in it's root form, when it is used in the preterit past, it is "Tay", and when it is used in the imperfect past, it is "Aktay". It is used differently than in English, and is better translated as "is pleasing to", meaning that the thing doing the liking is actually the object of the sentence rather than the subject.

The word for apples is borrowed from English in Tizin, and so it is "Apple" is the nominative form, "Zapple" is the accusative form, and "Izapple" is the genitive form. 

Please translate "I like apples" to Tizin.

The fictional language Tizin follows a Verb-Object-Subject word order. To refer to oneself, use "Pa" (nominative), "Mato" (accusative), or "Sing" (genitive). The verb for intense liking is "Maktay" in its present/root form, "Tay" in the preterit past, and "Aktay" in the imperfect past; importantly, it means "is pleasing to", so the thing being liked is the grammatical subject and the liker is the object. The word for apples is borrowed from English: "Apple" (nominative), "Zapple" (accusative), "Izapple" (genitive). Using these rules, how do you say "I like apples" in Tizin?

In Tizin, a made-up language, sentences are ordered Verb then direct object then subject. The first-person pronoun forms are: "Pa" (nominative), "Mato" (accusative), "Sing" (genitive). The verb meaning an intense fondness — best translated as "is pleasing to" — is "Maktay" in the present tense, "Tay" in the preterit past, and "Aktay" in the imperfect past; because of its meaning, the thing liked acts as the subject while the liker acts as the object. Apples, borrowed from English, appear as "Apple" (nominative), "Zapple" (accusative), and "Izapple" (genitive). Translate the sentence "I like apples" into Tizin.

Suppose you are learning the invented language Tizin, which places the verb first, then the direct object, then the subject. For yourself, the nominative form is "Pa", the accusative is "Mato", and the genitive is "Sing". There is a verb conveying a strong liking that functions as "is pleasing to" — its present/root form is "Maktay", its preterit past form is "Tay", and its imperfect past form is "Aktay"; because of this meaning, what is liked becomes the subject and the one who likes becomes the object. The Tizin word for apples is an English loanword: nominative "Apple", accusative "Zapple", genitive "Izapple". You want to tell your Tizin-speaking friend that you like apples. What is the correct Tizin translation of "I like apples"?

question: A standard Rubik’s cube has been broken into cubes making up its sides. The cubes are jumbled, and one is removed. There are 6 cubes with one colored face, 12 edge cubes with two colored faces, and 8 corner cubes with three colored faces. All blue cubes have been found. All cubes directly left, right, above, and below the orange center cube have been found, along with the center cube. The green corners have all been found, along with all green that borders yellow. For all orange cubes found, the opposite face’s cubes have been found. The removed cube has two colors on its faces. What are they? Answer using a comma separated list, with the colors ordered alphabetically.

A standard Rubik's cube has been disassembled into its individual pieces. The pieces are mixed up, and one has been taken away. There are 6 center pieces with one colored face, 12 edge pieces with two colored faces, and 8 corner pieces with three colored faces. Every blue piece has been recovered. Every piece directly to the left, right, above, and below the orange center piece has been recovered, including the orange center itself. All green corner pieces have been recovered, plus all green pieces that share a border with yellow. For every orange piece that has been recovered, all pieces on the opposite face have also been recovered. The missing piece has exactly two colors on its faces. What are those two colors? Provide your answer as a comma-separated list with the colors in alphabetical order.

Take a standard Rubik's cube and break it into all of its component cubelets. One cubelet is removed from the scrambled collection. Among the cubelets: 6 have exactly one colored face (centers), 12 have exactly two colored faces (edges), and 8 have exactly three colored faces (corners). The following cubelets have been accounted for: all blue cubelets; the orange center cubelet and all cubelets immediately left, right, above, and below it; all green corner cubelets and all green cubelets adjacent to yellow; and for each orange cubelet found, every cubelet on its opposite face. The missing cubelet is an edge piece. What two colors appear on its faces? List the colors alphabetically, separated by a comma.

Imagine a standard Rubik's cube taken apart into its constituent pieces, which are then shuffled and one is removed. The full set consists of 6 one-colored-face centers, 12 two-colored-face edges, and 8 three-colored-face corners. The pieces that have been located are: all pieces containing blue; the orange center and each piece immediately adjacent (left, right, above, below) to it; all green corner pieces and all green pieces that touch yellow; and for every orange piece found, the complete set of pieces on the face opposite to orange. The removed piece bears two colors. What are they? Answer with a comma-separated list ordered alphabetically.

You may not move fewer than two cells, and you may not move backward. You must avoid moving onto any blue cells. On the eleventh turn, what is the 6-digit hex code (without prefix) of the color of the cell where you land after moving?

Given this table defining * on the set S = {a, b, c, d, e}

|*|a|b|c|d|e|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|a|a|b|c|b|d|
|b|b|c|a|e|c|
|c|c|a|b|b|a|
|d|b|e|b|e|d|
|e|d|b|a|d|c|

provide the subset of S involved in any possible counter-examples that prove * is not commutative. Provide your answer as a comma separated list of the elements in the set in alphabetical order.

Using the table below that defines the binary operation * on S = {a, b, c, d, e}:

|*|a|b|c|d|e|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|a|a|b|c|b|d|
|b|b|c|a|e|c|
|c|c|a|b|b|a|
|d|b|e|b|e|d|
|e|d|b|a|d|c|

Identify all elements of S that appear in any counter-example demonstrating that * is not commutative. List the elements as a comma-separated list in alphabetical order.

The table below defines an operation * over the set S = {a, b, c, d, e}:

|*|a|b|c|d|e|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|a|a|b|c|b|d|
|b|b|c|a|e|c|
|c|c|a|b|b|a|
|d|b|e|b|e|d|
|e|d|b|a|d|c|

Which elements of S are part of at least one counter-example showing that * fails to be commutative? Give your answer as a comma-separated list in alphabetical order.

Consider the binary operation * defined on S = {a, b, c, d, e} by the following Cayley table:

|*|a|b|c|d|e|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|a|a|b|c|b|d|
|b|b|c|a|e|c|
|c|c|a|b|b|a|
|d|b|e|b|e|d|
|e|d|b|a|d|c|

Find the subset of S whose elements participate in counter-examples that witness the non-commutativity of *. Express your answer as a comma-separated list of those elements sorted in alphabetical order.

Hi, I'm making a pie but I could use some help with my shopping list. I have everything I need for the crust, but I'm not sure about the filling. Could you please listen to the recipe and list all of the ingredients that my friend described? I only want the ingredients for the filling, as I have everything I need to make my favorite pie crust. I've attached the recipe as Strawberry pie.mp3.

In your response, please only list the ingredients, not any measurements. So if the recipe calls for "a pinch of salt" or "two cups of ripe strawberries" the ingredients on the list would be "salt" and "ripe strawberries".

Please format your response as a comma separated list of ingredients. Also, please alphabetize the ingredients.
final_answer: cornstarch, freshly squeezed lemon juice, granulated sugar, pure vanilla extract, ripe strawberries

You are Van Helsing, a renowned vampire hunter. A Count of Moldova, Lațcu IV, son of  Costea, has tasked you with investigating the village of Șirnea in neighboring Wallachia. The Count's advisors have reported that a vampire was spotted crossing the border near the village, and would like you to investigate it.

You travel to the village of Șirnea, and you begin your investigation. One night, just before dawn, you catch a glimpse of a man in a long black cape with red lining leaping from roof-top to roof-top with superhuman agility. It's a vampire! You try to chase the creature back to its home, but the creature is too fast. However, because of the remoteness of the village, you know with absolute certainty that the vampire must be a resident of the village. You decide that your best course of action will be to visit all 100 residents of the town during the day. You know something about vampires and humans that will make your investigation possible; humans always tell the truth, but vampires always lie.

In the afternoon, you go from house to house, speaking with all 100 residents of Șirnea. You ask everyone the same question: "How many vampires are living in Șirnea". Everyone in the village gives the same response, "At least one of us is a human."

How many residents of Șirnea have been turned into vampires?

You are Van Helsing, a famous vampire hunter. Lațcu IV, Count of Moldova and son of Costea, has sent you to investigate the Wallachian village of Șirnea after his advisors reported a vampire crossing the border nearby.

Upon arriving, you spend several nights watching the village. Just before dawn one morning, you spot a figure in a long black cape with red lining bounding across rooftops with impossible speed and agility — unmistakably a vampire. You attempt pursuit, but the creature outruns you. Given how isolated Șirnea is, you are completely certain the vampire must be one of the village's 100 residents.

Your plan: visit every resident during daylight hours. You know the key distinction between the two kinds of beings — humans always speak the truth, while vampires always lie.

That afternoon, you knock on every door and pose the same question to each of the 100 residents: "How many vampires live in Șirnea?" Each and every resident gives you the identical answer: "At least one of us is a human."

Based on this, how many of Șirnea's 100 residents are vampires?

Playing the role of Van Helsing, the legendary vampire hunter, you have been commissioned by Lațcu IV — Count of Moldova, son of Costea — to look into strange happenings in the Wallachian village of Șirnea. Border scouts reported a vampire entering the region near that village.

After arriving in Șirnea, you keep watch through the night. Just before the break of dawn, you catch sight of a man wearing a long black cape lined in red, leaping between rooftops with supernatural speed. A vampire! Your chase proves fruitless — the creature is far too quick. Yet the village's remoteness assures you beyond any doubt that this vampire is among the 100 people who live there.

You resolve to interview every single resident during daylight. Your knowledge of the supernatural tells you that humans are incapable of lying, whereas vampires are incapable of telling the truth.

Over the course of the afternoon, you visit all 100 residents and ask each one the same question: "How many vampires are living in Șirnea?" Without exception, every resident responds: "At least one of us is a human."

How many of the 100 residents of Șirnea are vampires?

Imagine you are Van Helsing, the celebrated vampire hunter. The Count of Moldova, Lațcu IV, son of Costea, has dispatched you to the Wallachian village of Șirnea to investigate a report from his advisors that a vampire was seen crossing the border in that area.

Once in Șirnea, you conduct nighttime surveillance. In the hour just before dawn, you witness a caped figure — long black cloak with red lining — leaping from rooftop to rooftop with superhuman speed. You recognize it as a vampire and attempt to follow, but it escapes. Because the village is so remote, you are absolutely certain the vampire resides among Șirnea's 100 inhabitants.

You choose to call on every resident during the day. You rely on one crucial fact: humans invariably tell the truth, and vampires invariably lie.

That afternoon, you visit all 100 residents one by one, asking each the same question: "How many vampires are living in Șirnea?" Every single resident gives you the same answer: "At least one of us is a human."

Given this, what is the total number of vampires among Șirnea's 100 residents?

question: My family reunion is this week, and I was assigned the mashed potatoes to bring. The attendees include my married mother and father, my twin brother and his family, my aunt and her family, my grandma and her brother, her brother's daughter, and his daughter's family. All the adults but me have been married, and no one is divorced or remarried, but my grandpa and my grandma's sister-in-law passed away last year. All living spouses are attending. My brother has two children that are still kids, my aunt has one six-year-old, and my grandma's brother's daughter has three kids under 12. I figure each adult will eat about 1.5 potatoes of mashed potatoes and each kid will eat about 1/2 a potato of mashed potatoes, except my second cousins don't eat carbs. The average potato is about half a pound, and potatoes are sold in 5-pound bags. How many whole bags of potatoes do I need? Just give the number.

I have a family reunion coming up this week and I'm in charge of bringing mashed potatoes. Here's who's attending: my mom and dad (married), my twin brother along with his family, my aunt and her family, my grandma and her brother, his daughter, and her daughter's family. Every adult except me has been married, with no divorces or remarriages, though my grandpa and my grandma's sister-in-law both passed away last year. All surviving spouses will be there. My brother has two young kids, my aunt has one six-year-old child, and my grandma's brother's daughter has three children under 12. For portions, each adult will eat roughly 1.5 potatoes worth of mashed potatoes and each kid about half a potato, except my second cousins don't eat carbs. A typical potato weighs about half a pound, and potatoes come in 5-pound bags. How many whole bags of potatoes do I need to buy? Just give the number.

This week is my family reunion and I've been asked to make mashed potatoes. The people coming are: my married parents, my twin brother and his family, my aunt and her family, my grandma and her brother, that brother's daughter, and her family. All adults except me have been married — nobody is divorced or remarried — but my grandpa and my grandma's sister-in-law died last year. Every living spouse is attending. My brother's two children are still kids, my aunt has a six-year-old, and my grandma's brother's daughter has three kids all under 12. I'm planning on 1.5 potatoes per adult and half a potato per child, but my second cousins skip carbs entirely. The average potato is about half a pound, and bags at the store contain 5 pounds of potatoes. How many complete bags do I need to purchase? Give just the number.

Our family reunion is happening this week and I've been tasked with bringing mashed potatoes. The guest list includes my mom and dad (who are married to each other), my twin brother and his family, my aunt and her family, my grandma and her brother, her brother's daughter, and that daughter's family. Every adult attending except me has been married, and there are no divorces or remarriages, although my grandpa and my grandma's sister-in-law passed away last year — all other spouses are coming. My brother has two kids who are still children, my aunt has one child who is six years old, and my grandma's brother's daughter has three children all younger than 12. I'm estimating 1.5 potatoes per adult and half a potato per kid, with the exception that my second cousins don't eat carbs. Potatoes average about half a pound each, and they're sold in 5-pound bags. How many whole bags of potatoes will I need? Answer with just the number.

Bob was invited to participate in a game show, and he advanced to the final round. The final round offered Bob the chance to win a large sum by playing a game against the host. The host has 30 shiny prop coins, each of which is worth $1,000 if Bob manages to win them by playing the game. The host hides the coins in three different prize boxes and then shuffles their order. The only rule restricting the host's coin placement is that one box must contain at least 2 coins, and one box must contain 6 more coins than another box. In order to play, Bob must submit three guesses, one guess for the number of coins in each box. The box is then opened and the number of coins is revealed. If Bob's guess is a number greater than the number of coins in the box, Bob earns no coins. If Bob guesses a number equal to or less than the number of coins in the box, Bob wins a number of coins equal to his guess.

If Bob plays uses the optimal strategy, what's the minimum amount of money he can win from the game?

Bob has made it to the final round of a game show, where he faces the host in a coin-guessing game. The host distributes 30 shiny prop coins (each worth $1,000) among three prize boxes, then shuffles them. The placement must satisfy two rules: at least one box contains a minimum of 2 coins, and one box must have exactly 6 more coins than another box. Bob submits one guess per box before it is opened. If his guess exceeds the actual number of coins in a box, he wins nothing from that box. If his guess is less than or equal to the actual count, he wins coins equal to his guess. Assuming Bob plays optimally, what is the least amount of money he is guaranteed to win?

In the final round of a game show, Bob gets to play a guessing game against the host involving 30 prop coins each valued at $1,000. The host secretly places all 30 coins into three boxes and shuffles them, subject to two constraints: one box must hold at least 2 coins, and one box must contain exactly 6 more coins than some other box. Bob then submits three guesses — one for each box. For each box, if Bob's guess is strictly greater than the coins inside, he receives nothing from it; if his guess is equal to or less than the coins inside, he receives coins equal to his guess. What is the minimum dollar amount Bob can win when following an optimal strategy?

Bob has reached the final stage of a game show, where the host conceals 30 shiny prop coins across three prize boxes and randomizes their order. Each coin is worth $1,000. The host's distribution must obey two conditions: one box must contain no fewer than 2 coins, and the number of coins in one box must be exactly 6 more than in another box. Bob must guess the coin count for each box before it is revealed. A guess that is higher than the actual coin count yields $0 from that box; a guess that equals or falls below the actual count earns Bob coins matching his guess. If Bob employs the optimal guessing strategy, what is the minimum amount of money he could end up winning?

Here's a fun riddle that I think you'll enjoy.

You have been selected to play the final round of the hit new game show "Pick That Ping-Pong". In this round, you will be competing for a large cash prize. Your job will be to pick one of several different numbered ping-pong balls, and then the game will commence. The host describes how the game works.

A device consisting of a winding clear ramp and a series of pistons controls the outcome of the game. The ramp feeds balls onto a platform. The platform has room for three ping-pong balls at a time. The three balls on the platform are each aligned with one of three pistons. At each stage of the game, one of the three pistons will randomly fire, ejecting the ball it strikes. If the piston ejects the ball in the first position on the platform the balls in the second and third position on the platform each advance one space, and the next ball on the ramp advances to the third position. If the piston ejects the ball in the second position, the ball in the first position is released and rolls away, the ball in the third position advances two spaces to occupy the first position, and the next two balls on the ramp advance to occupy the second and third positions on the platform. If the piston ejects the ball in the third position, the ball in the first position is released and rolls away, the ball in the second position advances one space to occupy the first position, and the next two balls on the ramp advance to occupy the second and third positions on the platform.

The ramp begins with 100 numbered ping-pong balls, arranged in ascending order from 1 to 100. The host activates the machine and the first three balls, numbered 1, 2, and 3, advance to the platform. Before the random firing of the pistons begins, you are asked which of the 100 balls you would like to pick. If your pick is ejected by one of the pistons, you win the grand prize, $10,000.

Which ball should you choose to maximize your odds of winning the big prize? Please provide your answer as the number of the ball selected.

Here's a probability puzzle for you to consider.

You've been chosen to participate in the final round of a popular new game show called "Pick That Ping-Pong". A large cash prize is on the line. Your task is to select one of several numbered ping-pong balls before the game begins. The host explains the rules.

A machine made up of a winding clear ramp and a set of pistons determines the game's outcome. The ramp feeds balls onto a platform that holds exactly three ping-pong balls at a time. Each ball on the platform is aligned with one of three pistons. At every stage, one of the three pistons fires at random, ejecting the ball it hits. If the first-position ball is ejected, the balls in positions two and three each move forward one space, and the next ball on the ramp fills the third position. If the second-position ball is ejected, the ball in the first position is released and rolls away, the ball in the third position jumps two spaces to occupy the first position, and the next two ramp balls fill positions two and three. If the third-position ball is ejected, the ball in the first position is released and rolls away, the ball in the second position moves forward one space to occupy the first position, and the next two ramp balls fill positions two and three.

The ramp starts loaded with 100 numbered ping-pong balls in ascending order from 1 to 100. The machine is activated and balls 1, 2, and 3 move onto the platform. Before any piston fires, you must choose which of the 100 balls you want. If the piston ejects your chosen ball, you win the grand prize of $10,000.

Which ball should you pick to maximize your probability of winning? Please provide your answer as the number of the ball selected.

Consider the following game show scenario.

You have been selected as a contestant in the final round of the new hit game show "Pick That Ping-Pong", where you can win a large cash prize. You must choose one numbered ping-pong ball from a set, and then the machine runs. The host explains how the machine operates.

The machine consists of a winding clear ramp that feeds balls onto a three-slot platform, along with three pistons—one aligned with each slot. During each stage, one piston fires at random and ejects the ball in its slot. When the ball in slot one is ejected, the balls in slots two and three advance one slot each, and the next ball from the ramp enters slot three. When the ball in slot two is ejected, the ball in slot one is released and rolls away, the ball in slot three advances two slots to slot one, and the next two balls from the ramp fill slots two and three. When the ball in slot three is ejected, the ball in slot one is released and rolls away, the ball in slot two advances one slot to slot one, and the next two balls from the ramp fill slots two and three.

The ramp is loaded with 100 ping-pong balls numbered 1 through 100 in ascending order. When the machine starts, balls 1, 2, and 3 are placed on the platform. You must choose your ball before the pistons begin firing. If your chosen ball gets ejected by a piston, you win the grand prize of $10,000.

To maximize your chances of winning, which ball should you select? Please provide your answer as the number of the ball selected.

Here is a game show riddle for you.

You've won a spot in the final round of the brand-new hit game show "Pick That Ping-Pong". You're competing for a big cash prize. The way it works: you pick one numbered ping-pong ball, and if a piston ejects that ball during the game, you win $10,000. The host describes the machine.

The apparatus features a winding clear ramp that feeds ping-pong balls onto a platform. The platform accommodates three balls simultaneously, each aligned with one of three pistons. At each step, one of the pistons randomly activates and ejects the ball it is aligned with. If the ejected ball was in position one, the balls in positions two and three each shift forward by one position, and the ramp feeds one new ball into position three. If the ejected ball was in position two, the ball in position one rolls away and exits, the ball in position three shifts forward two positions to position one, and two new balls from the ramp occupy positions two and three. If the ejected ball was in position three, the ball in position one rolls away and exits, the ball in position two shifts forward one position to position one, and two new balls from the ramp occupy positions two and three.

The ramp holds 100 ping-pong balls numbered in ascending order from 1 to 100. At the start, the machine advances balls 1, 2, and 3 onto the platform. You are asked to pick your ball before the pistons start firing randomly. If your chosen ball is ejected, you claim the $10,000 grand prize.

Which ball number gives you the best odds of winning? Please provide your answer as the number of the ball selected.
